data.title: UBS sees containerboard rebound in 2026, favors International Paper, Smurfit WestRock
data.description:
UBS analysts are growing more constructive on the North American containerboard sector heading into 2026, citing tightening supply, improving demand fundamentals and the potential for a successful price increase after a weak 2025.

In a Jan. 14 outlook report, UBS said unprecedented capacity reductions across the industry -- roughly 4 million tons, or about 12% of 2024 demand -- should help support at least one containerboard price hike in 2026. The firm forecasts a $40-per-ton increase in the second quarter, arguing that pricing skepticism tied to soft box shipments and weak recycled fiber prices may be overstated.

UBS expects volumes to stabilize and gradually recover next year as lower interest rates and business- and consumer-friendly tax policies lift manufacturing activity and spending. The firm’s proprietary “UBS Corrugator” model points to roughly 70 basis points of year-over-year box shipment growth in 2026, with industrial production and consumer demand acting as the primary drivers.

Stock picks

Against that backdrop, UBS reiterated Buy ratings on International Paper (IP) and Smurfit WestRock (SW) (SMFTF) (SMFKY), calling both stocks its preferred ways to play a containerboard recovery.

For International Paper (IP), UBS highlighted self-help initiatives and cost savings following recent mill closures, as well as the company’s long-term target of $5 billion in earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization by 2027. The bank set a $51 price target on the stock, implying roughly 20% upside from current levels even under conservative margin assumptions.

Smurfit WestRock, meanwhile, is seen benefiting from $400 million in announced synergies, with potential for another $400 million over time. UBS noted that upcoming strategy updates could provide greater clarity on execution and incremental savings.

By contrast, UBS struck a more cautious tone on consumer boxboard, maintaining Neutral ratings on Graphic Packaging Holding (GPK) and Sonoco Products (SON).

The firm warned that boxboard markets remain oversupplied, with additional pressure coming from the expansion of GLP-1 weight-loss drugs, particularly in pill form, which UBS believes could weigh on packaged food demand. For Graphic Packaging (GPK), UBS flagged pricing risk tied to historically low spreads between coated recycled board and substitute grades, alongside volume headwinds as the company ramps a new mill in Waco, Texas.

Sonoco (SON) is expected to benefit from prior footprint rationalization and price increases in industrial boxboard, but UBS noted that divestitures and portfolio shifts could offset those gains in 2026, resulting in modest earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization pressure year over year.

Overall, UBS said the balance of risks in 2026 favors containerboard producers over boxboard names, with supply discipline and macro tailwinds creating a more supportive environment for pricing and earnings recovery than investors currently expect.

data.title: H.B. Fuller targets $630M–$660M adjusted EBITDA for 2026 while advancing margin expansion strategy
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: H.B. Fuller Company (FUL) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Celeste Mastin opened by stating, "Our execution and agility in the quarter and throughout the year generated double-digit EPS growth and EBITDA at the top end of our full year guidance range amidst an unpredictable economic backdrop and challenging demand landscape." Mastin highlighted the company's focus on material optionality, flexibility, and maintaining global reliability, strengthening partnerships and enhancing competitive positioning. She emphasized, "We are exiting the fourth quarter with strong momentum heading into 2026 and are firmly on track to achieve our target of greater than 20% EBITDA margin."
  • Mastin reported net revenue was down 3.1%, with a 1% increase after adjusting for the Flooring divestiture. Organic growth was down 1.3%, volume down 2.5%, and pricing up 1.2%. EBITDA reached $170 million, up 15% year-on-year, with a 19% EBITDA margin, a 290 basis point expansion.
  • In segment performance, HHC's organic revenue was down 1.8% but EBITDA rose nearly 30% year-on-year. Engineering Adhesives organic revenue increased 2.2%, with automotive, electronics, and aerospace cited for strength. BAS organic sales decreased 4.8%. Geographic highlights included solid Asia Pacific growth and EIMEA softness in packaging and construction.
  • Mastin described the launch of the Quantum Leap initiative to consolidate manufacturing and warehouses, improving cost structure. She also noted recent acquisitions in medical adhesives and fastener coating systems, stating, "These businesses have performed exceptionally well with revenue up approximately 15% versus pre-acquisition 2024 and EBITDA up almost 30%."
  • CFO John Corkrean stated, "Adjusted gross profit margin of 32.5% increased 290 basis points year-on-year. The impact of pricing, raw material cost actions, acquisitions and divestitures and targeted cost reduction efforts drove the year-on-year increase in adjusted gross profit margin." Corkrean also highlighted adjusted EBITDA of $170 million, up 14.6% year-on-year, and adjusted EPS of $1.28, up 39% versus Q4 2024. Cash flow from operations reached $107 million, up 25% year-on-year.

Outlook

  • For fiscal 2026, H.B. Fuller anticipates full year net revenue to be flat to up 2% versus 2025. Organic revenue is expected to be approximately flat, with foreign currency translation projected to add about 1%. Adjusted EBITDA is guided between $630 million and $660 million. Adjusted EPS guidance is $4.35 to $4.70. Operating cash flow is expected between $275 million and $300 million, with capital expenditures of approximately $160 million, including $50 million for Project Quantum Leap.
  • First quarter 2026 revenue is expected to be down low single digits, with adjusted EBITDA between $110 million and $120 million. Management attributed the Q1 outlook primarily to the timing of Chinese New Year, which will shift 1 to 2 weeks of revenue and EBITDA into Q2.

Financial Results

  • Net revenue for Q4 2025 was down 3.1% year-on-year. Adjusted gross profit margin increased to 32.5%. Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was $170 million. Fourth quarter adjusted EPS was $1.28. Cash flow from operations was $107 million, and net working capital as a percentage of annualized net revenue was 15.8%.
  • Net debt to adjusted EBITDA improved to 3.1x from 3.3x at the end of Q3. The company reported repurchasing approximately one million shares in fiscal 2025.
  • Segment highlights: HHC EBITDA margin improved to 17.5%, Engineering Adhesives EBITDA margin to 23.5%, and BAS EBITDA decreased 7% year-on-year.

Q&A

  • Michael Harrison, Seaport Research Partners, asked about Q1 guidance and the impact of Chinese New Year. Mastin responded, "The biggest impact in Q1, Mike, is going to be Chinese New Year. So the timing of Chinese New Year in Q1 will result in some of that revenue being pushed into Q2."
  • Ghansham Panjabi, Baird, inquired about BAS and packaging drivers. Mastin explained, "The construction environment continues to weaken. That said, there’s some pretty exciting things going on in BAS...our glass business continues to succeed. Our 4SG product grew 18% in 2025 despite a reduction of housing starts of 6%." For packaging, Mastin described ongoing weakness in North America and growth in developing markets due to new capacity in Cairo and innovations in Asia.
  • Kevin McCarthy, Vertical Research Partners, asked about free cash flow outlook. Corkrean stated, "We guided to $275 million to $300 million versus $263 million this year...operating cash flow driven by income and working capital remaining a little higher in the near term."
  • Jeffrey Zekauskas, JPMorgan, questioned other income, deferred taxes, and solar market impact. Corkrean attributed other income to higher pension income and FX hedging gains. Mastin said, "In 2025, we had about $80 million of revenue in the solar business. What we're going to see is that's going to ramp down to around $50 million by the conclusion of this year."
  • Patrick Cunningham, Citi, asked about EA volume growth. Mastin stated, "Excluding solar, about 7% organic growth in the fourth quarter, 5% volume growth."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on the sustainability of margin expansion, segment-specific challenges, and the impact of external events such as Chinese New Year, with a neutral to slightly cautious tone, seeking clarity on volume growth and cash flow.
  • Management maintained a confident but measured tone throughout, emphasizing self-help actions and resilience. Mastin stated, "We’re expecting no macro help. We’ve built in a strong self-help approach to the year, much like we had to do last year."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst sentiment remained cautious, while management's confidence was more pronounced in highlighting margin progress and strategic initiatives.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q4 2025 guidance was more detailed, with EBITDA targeted between $630 million and $660 million for 2026, compared to a prior range of $615 million to $625 million for 2025. EPS guidance increased to $4.35–$4.70 from the previous $4.10–$4.25. Management continued to focus on margin expansion and cost reduction, with Quantum Leap and M&A providing new growth platforms. Analyst questions in Q4 were more granular about timing effects and segment-specific performance, while management emphasized resilience and execution over macro support. The tone from management was incrementally more constructive versus Q3, reflecting strengthened profitability and a clearer M&A pipeline.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited a challenging economic environment, ongoing geopolitical tensions, tariff uncertainty, elevated inflation, interest rates, and labor constraints as factors expected to weigh on manufacturing investment in 2026.
  • The company is addressing these risks through portfolio shifts, cost management, Quantum Leap consolidation, and continued pricing and sourcing actions. Analysts highlighted concerns about construction and packaging market weakness, free cash flow conversion, solar market headwinds, and the impact of Chinese New Year on Q1 results.

Final Takeaway

H.B. Fuller management communicated that the company enters 2026 with strong momentum, supported by disciplined cost actions, ongoing portfolio transformation, and a targeted approach to margin expansion. Despite persistent macro challenges, the company projects another year of profit growth, expects to achieve a greater than 20% EBITDA margin, and is leveraging recent acquisitions and the Quantum Leap initiative to further enhance its financial and competitive position.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Netflix signs worldwide pact to carry Sony films following their theater release
data.description:
Streaming giant Netflix Inc. (NFLX) and Sony Pictures Entertainment (SONY) on Thursday struck an exclusive multi-year worldwide Pay-1 licensing deal, under which Sony's feature films will stream on Netflix worldwide once their full theatrical release and home entertainment windows close. 

Financial terms of the agreement were not disclosed, but media reports suggest the deal is worth more than $7B.

The new global Pay-1 arrangement will roll out gradually starting later this year as individual territory rights become available, with full global availability on Netflix in early 2029, the companies said in a joint statement.

As part of the deal, Netflix will also license rights to select Sony feature film and television library titles. 

Nintendo’s live-action The Legend of Zelda, Spider-Man: Beyond the Spider-Verse, and Academy Award winner Sam Mendes’ quartet of Beatles films are among some of the first Sony feature film titles that will stream on Netflix under the new deal.

data.title: Energy Fuels sees significant annual EBITDA, lower than expected capex from White Mesa project
data.description:
Energy Fuels (UUUU) +3.2% in Thursday’s trading after saying the bankable feasibility study for the Phase 2 rare earth element processing expansion at its White Mesa Mill in Utah confirmed the expansion's "exceptional economics, competitive cost structure and ability to supply a significant share of U.S. rare earth demand."

Energy Fuels (UUUU) said the study estimates a $410M initial capital cost for the Phase 2 circuit, which would raise the company's production capability from the current 1K metric tons/year of NdPr oxide to more than 6K tons/year, along with 66 tons/year of terbium and 240 tons/year of dysprosium.

The BFS said the expansion has a projected after-tax net present value of $1.9B at an 8% discount rate and a 33% internal rate of return, with estimated average $311M annual EBITDA for the first 15 years from the Phase 2 circuit alone.

The company said it expects regulatory approval for the Phase 2 circuit by mid-2027, with planned construction and commissioning by Q1 2029.

data.title: BlackRock targets $400B in private markets fundraising by 2030 as platform integration fuels double-digit growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: BlackRock, Inc. (BLK) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Laurence Fink, CEO, opened by stating BlackRock is entering 2026 "with accelerating momentum across our entire platform" and highlighted the firm's "strongest year and quarter of net inflows in our history," reporting nearly $700 billion in new assets for 2025, including $342 billion in the fourth quarter. Fink emphasized the breadth of BlackRock's business, spanning public and private markets, technology, and client channels, and announced an "ambitious 2026 fundraising plan diversified across infrastructure, equity and debt, private financing solutions and multi-alternatives."
  • Fink pointed to BlackRock's leadership in foundational businesses like iShares, noting "iShares remains the market leader in ETFs in terms of organic assets and base fee growth, countries served and in product lineup." He also highlighted growth in emerging trends such as "private markets to wealth, 401(k)s, tokenization and private market data."
  • Martin Small, CFO, reported "Full year revenue of $24 billion was up 19% year-over-year. Operating income of $9.6 billion was up 18% and earnings per share of $48.09 increased 10%." Small added, "Quarterly operating income of $2.8 billion was up 22%, while earnings per share of $13.16 increased 10% versus a year ago."
  • Small described BlackRock’s dividend and buyback policy: "Our 10% increase to our 2026 dividend per share and increase in planned share repurchases to $1.8 billion are driven by our accelerating growth trajectory and platform success in 2025."
  • BlackRock’s Board approved a 10% increase to the first quarter 2026 dividend per share and authorized the repurchase of an additional 7 million shares.

Outlook

  • Management set a target to "raise a cumulative $400 billion in private markets by 2030."
  • Fink stated, "We plan to bring all the building blocks to serve wealth investors through coordinated multi-alts portfolios."
  • Small projected a "mid-single-digit percentage increase in G&A" for 2026, expecting BlackRock's headcount to be broadly flat next year.
  • The company enters 2026 with a base fees run rate "approximately 35% higher than our base fees in 2024 and approximately 50% higher than 2023," according to Small.

Financial Results

  • Full year revenue of $24 billion, operating income of $9.6 billion, and earnings per share of $48.09 were all highlighted as significant increases from the previous year.
  • Fourth quarter revenue reached $7 billion, operating income was $2.8 billion, and earnings per share amounted to $13.16.
  • Fourth quarter base fees and securities lending revenue totaled $5.3 billion, up 19% year-over-year.
  • Performance fees for the fourth quarter were $754 million, including $158 million from HPS.
  • Technology services and subscription revenue increased 24% year-over-year, with Preqin contributing $65 million in the fourth quarter.
  • Annual contract value grew 31% year-over-year, including the impact of Preqin.
  • Net inflows for 2025 totaled $698 billion, with iShares setting a new record at $527 billion.
  • Cash management saw $74 billion of net inflows in the fourth quarter and $131 billion for the full year.

Q&A

  • Craig Siegenthaler, BofA Securities: Asked about the net flow pipeline and the money market business with anticipated Fed cuts. Martin Small responded, "Organic base fee growth continues to outperform our 5-plus percent baseline target, 10% in Q3, 12% print in Q4, 9% for the year," and discussed the structural breadth of BlackRock’s growth. Fink added, "As global capital markets grow, cash is going to grow alongside of it."
  • Michael Cyprys, Morgan Stanley: Queried about Asia expansion. Fink explained, "Asia capital markets grew faster than the U.S. capital markets... the opportunity to develop a self-directed retirement platform in India is real."
  • Michael Brown, UBS: Discussed the insurance channel. Small noted, "We're the largest insurance company general account manager in the industry with $700 billion in assets... HPS also manages over $60 billion of credit assets for over 125 insurance companies."
  • Alexander Blostein, Goldman Sachs: Asked about operating margins. Small answered, "Excluding the impact of performance fees and related comp, our margin would have been 45.5%, up 30 basis points." He stated the target remains "45% or higher greater adjusted operating margin."
  • Kenneth Worthington, JPMorgan: Asked about Preqin and private market data. Small described, "We're working on building investable indices that we hope to bring to market here in the next few years."
  • Daniel Fannon, Jefferies: Inquired about private credit flows and outlook. Small disclosed, "We had $7 billion of private credit net inflows in the quarter, primarily due to deployment activity."
  • Benjamin Budish, Barclays: Asked about wealth channel expectations and product rollout. Small detailed upcoming "H Series of vehicles" for private wealth and retail channels and a goal to grow the private markets to wealth series to at least $60 billion of AUM by 2030.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a positive and congratulatory tone, frequently remarking on record growth and asking for details on sustainability, expansion plans, and key business drivers.
  • Management's responses were confident and upbeat during prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently referencing "momentum," "ambition," and "confidence in our model." Notably, Fink stated "We are confident in our organic base fee growth ambitions" and Small described "real opportunity to drive margin expansion."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management showed increased enthusiasm, with more explicit forward-looking statements and higher financial targets being communicated.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 is more ambitious, with a raised dividend and a substantial increase in share repurchase plans compared to the previous quarter.
  • The fourth quarter delivered higher organic base fee growth (12%) and record net inflows compared to strong but slightly lower levels in Q3.
  • Management emphasized platform integration (GIP, HPS, Preqin) and its impact on margins and product breadth, while reiterating long-term targets for private markets fundraising and margin expansion.
  • Analyst focus shifted from integration progress and new mandates in Q3 to scalability, operating leverage, and international expansion in Q4.
  • Sentiment moved from strong optimism to even greater confidence, with management providing more detailed forward guidance.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged "total expense increased 19% in 2025, primarily driven by higher compensation, sales, asset and account expense and G&A expense."
  • Small flagged "a mid-single-digit percentage increase in G&A" for 2026 as a consideration for cost management.
  • On private credit, Small said, "default rates are rising but they remain in historical ranges," and noted the need for vigilance with smaller borrowers likely to face more challenges.
  • Fink commented on potential future regulatory and market risks, particularly regarding the inclusion of private markets in retirement funds.

Final Takeaway

BlackRock closed 2025 with record-breaking net inflows, robust organic base fee growth, and significant expansion across technology, private markets, and global client channels. The integration of GIP, HPS, and Preqin is underscoring the platform’s ability to capture growth opportunities, while new fundraising and product initiatives are set to drive further expansion. Management projects continued margin expansion, record dividends and buybacks, and targets $400 billion in private markets fundraising by 2030, with momentum and confidence carrying into 2026 and beyond.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: U.S. megabanks reduce headcount by most in nearly a decade - report
data.description:
The six largest Wall Street banks, including JPMorgan Chase (JPM), Bank of America (BAC), Wells Fargo (WFC), Goldman Sachs (GS) and Morgan Stanley (MS), cut their aggregate headcount last year by the most since 2016, according to a Thursday media report.

The lenders had a combined 1.09M workers at the end of December, down some 10.6K from a year ago to the lowest level since 2021, Bloomberg reported. Many big firms hired aggressively during the 2020-2021 deal boom, before having to pull back after a slowdown in 2022. Now, AI-driven automation and efficiency gains are starting to reduce the need to rebuild headcount by letting banks do the same work with fewer people.

During Wells Fargo's (WFC) Q3 earnings call, CEO and Chairman Charles Scharf said the bank's focus on managing expenses has helped with business growth, noting its headcount is down over 25% since Q2 2020.

Citigroup (C), amid its business revamp, was said to have finished 2025 with 3K fewer employees than it had at the end of 2024. The bank is set to cut 1K jobs this week, and CEO Jane Fraser signaled additional layoffs to come later this year.

Some firms grew their headcount from a year earlier, including Goldman (GS) (+2% Y/Y to 47.4K employees) and Morgan Stanley (MS) (+2.5K), Bloomberg reported. JPMorgan (JPM) also added jobs last year, albeit at the slowest clip since the onset of the pandemic.

Meanwhile, BofA (BAC) Chairman and CEO Brian Moynihan said during the lender's post-earnings call that he sees the number of employees at the bank to fall this year as it leans on attrition.

data.title: Taiwan to invest at least $250B in semiconductors, energy, AI as part of US trade deal
data.description:
The U.S. and Taiwan announced a new trade deal on Thursday, with the Asian nation investing significant sums of money in the U.S. In return, the U.S. will cut tariffs on products from Taiwan.

As part of the deal, Taiwan will invest at least $250B in semiconductors, energy and artificial intelligence production in the U.S., the White House said in a release. The Asian nation will also provide credit guarantees of at least $250B for additional investment from Taiwanese companies, including “supporting the establishment and expansion of the full semiconductor supply chain and ecosystem in the United States.”

In return, the U.S. cut its reciprocal tariff rate applied on Taiwanese goods to no more than 15%. Tariffs coming from Section 232 on goods such as Taiwanese auto parts, timber, lumber, and wood derivative products will total no more than 15%.

The U.S. also got rid of its reciprocal tariff for generic pharmaceuticals, generic ingredients, aircraft components, and unavailable natural resources.

In addition, the White House said that Taiwanese companies who have completed new chip production projects in the U.S. “will still be able to import 1.5 times their new U.S. production capacity without paying Section 232 duties.”

Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), Taiwan's largest company by market cap, saw its shares rise nearly 5% on Thursday after the global foundry reported better-than-expected quarterly results.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: First Horizon signals 3–7% revenue growth for 2026 while maintaining flattish expense outlook
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: First Horizon Corporation (FHN) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO D. Jordan highlighted "significant progress in delivering value for our clients, associates and shareholders," with increased pre-provision net revenue and return on tangible common equity, which reached 15% in the back half of 2025. Jordan stated, "Loan and deposit trends were solid, and we improved balance sheet profitability through a better loan mix and pricing, disciplined control of deposit costs and tighter integration of deposits within our client relationships."
  • Jordan also noted, "In 2025, we also returned just under $900 million of capital and stock repurchases and just over $300 million in dividends." He added, "We are optimistic about our ability to improve profitability and continue to grow earnings in 2026."
  • CFO Hope Dmuchowski reported, "We ended the year with a strong fourth quarter that includes earnings per share of $0.52, net interest margin of 3.51% and 2% loan growth."
  • Dmuchowski emphasized quarterly deposit growth, stating, "Period-end balances increased by $2 billion compared to prior quarter. The average rate paid on interest-bearing deposits decreased to 2.53%, coming down from the third quarter average of 2.78%."
  • Dmuchowski announced, "We bought back just under $335 million of common shares, bringing our full year total to $894 million. We also announced a new repurchase program of $1.2 billion at the end of October, and we currently have just under $1 billion of authorization remaining."
  • On strategic priorities, Jordan said, "We instilled our strategic plan into a 5-page framework to provide clarity for all of our associates about how we differentiate in the marketplace and create broad, deep, long-lasting client relationships."

Outlook

  • Dmuchowski detailed the 2026 outlook: "We once again expect year-over-year PPNR growth with mid-single-digit balance sheet growth and positive operating leverage. Our total revenue expectations range from 3% to 7% growth year-over-year, which accounts for a variety of interest rate and business mix scenarios."
  • Dmuchowski stated, "Our expense outlook remains flattish with the exception of incremental incentive expenses associated with higher countercyclical revenue."
  • Net charge-off expectation for 2026 is "15 to 25 basis points," and the CET1 target is set to fluctuate "between 10.5% and 10.75% with loan growth throughout the year."
  • Management confirmed, "We still see $100 million in additional opportunity and expect to make significant progress on that in 2026 and 2027."

Financial Results

  • Dmuchowski reported, "Earnings per share of $0.52, net interest margin of 3.51% and 2% loan growth."
  • Net interest income expanded by approximately $2 million, with deposit pricing as the largest benefit. Interest-bearing deposit costs declined by 25 basis points.
  • Loans to mortgage companies increased $776 million from the prior quarter, and C&I portfolio balances increased $727 million. CRE portfolio paydowns slowed, with a $111 million reduction.
  • Fee income rose by $3 million, largely driven by $4.4 million in equipment finance lease business activity.
  • Adjusted expenses increased $4 million, with personnel expenses up $12 million due to incentives and annual bonus adjustments.
  • Net charge-offs increased by $4 million to $30 million; the net charge-off ratio was 19 basis points. No provision for credit losses was recorded; ACL to loan ratio declined to 1.31%.
  • CET1 ended at 10.64% following capital deployment into loan growth and share buybacks.

Q&A

  • Casey Haire, Autonomous Research: Asked about the 3% to 7% revenue outlook and major wildcards. Dmuchowski responded, "Our base case kind of middle of the range is the current forward curve...if we were able to exceed that, you'd be at the higher range."
  • Haire also questioned expense normalization post-tech investment. Dmuchowski explained, "We really start with a base case of expenses being in line with inflation...which would be 2.5% to 3% currently."
  • Ryan Nash, Goldman Sachs: Inquired about loan growth drivers and deposit growth. Dmuchowski said, "We are expecting [mortgage warehouse] to pick up...we think C&I has hit that inflect point."
  • Dmuchowski on deposit growth: "We have a lot of initiatives...primarily our new treasury management system...We've seen great momentum in treasury management in the back half of the year."
  • John Pancari, Evercore: Sought detail on NII vs. fee growth. Dmuchowski outlined, "If we can get that gain on sale back up...we saw a more normalized resale activity." Jordan added, "We have a very well-balanced business model and that we have the countercyclical businesses."
  • Pancari also asked about M&A priorities. Jordan clarified, "That's not a priority for us. Our priority is delivering higher returns, increased profitability and leveraging the franchise and the footprint that we have."
  • Bernard Von Gizycki, Deutsche Bank: Asked about ROTCE sustainability and reserve outlook. Dmuchowski said, "I think we've hit that sustained number on a go-forward basis." On reserves, Hung remarked, "Momentum we've had in those non-cost resolutions, that's why we have been able to have the other reserve releases."
  • Jared Shaw, Barclays: Questioned capital deployment and buyback appetite. Jordan affirmed, "We will participate in buybacks when appropriate."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on the durability of revenue growth, expense discipline, loan growth, and capital deployment, displaying a neutral to slightly positive tone, seeking clarity on sustainability and upside potential.
  • Management conveyed confidence in operational progress, profitability, and growth targets, using phrases like "we are optimistic," "I'm confident," and "we feel very good about the sustainable nature of the progress that we've made."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management maintained a confident and constructive tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, with analysts remaining focused on the sustainability of recent improvements.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue guidance moved from "flat to up 4%" to "3% to 7% growth" for 2026.
  • Expense outlook remains "flattish," consistent with prior commentary, but now more explicitly tied to revenue-driven incentives.
  • Both quarters featured discussion on mid-single-digit loan growth, but management highlighted improved loan and deposit trends this quarter.
  • Capital return increased, with nearly $900 million in repurchases for 2025 and a new $1.2 billion program announced.
  • ROTCE target of 15% was hit for two consecutive quarters, with management now expressing belief in its ongoing sustainability.
  • Analysts' focus shifted from M&A speculation to questions about organic growth, pipeline momentum, and the mechanics of revenue and expense performance.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited macroeconomic uncertainty as a key variable, particularly regarding deposit costs and the Fed's balance sheet.
  • Dmuchowski noted, "The #1 thing that concerns me...is what happens with the Fed's balance sheet."
  • Loan growth outlook is described as disciplined, with management emphasizing conservative underwriting and client selection.
  • Credit risks remain a focus, but reserve levels are described as robust, with management noting a "balanced view of the economy."
  • Analysts continued to probe for clarity on potential M&A activity, expense normalization, and growth sustainability, but management reaffirmed priorities on profitability and organic expansion.

Final Takeaway

Management underscored progress across profitability, capital return, and balance sheet optimization, projecting 3% to 7% revenue growth and flattish expenses for 2026. The company aims to sustain a 15% ROTCE, deepen client relationships, and invest in technology and talent, while maintaining a disciplined approach to credit and capital deployment. The outlook is supported by robust momentum in commercial and mortgage business lines and a strong capital base, positioning First Horizon to capitalize on growth opportunities through 2026 and beyond.
